speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 2, Sari Baron being interviewed by Betsy Stern, November 5, 1996. You were telling us you had just arrived at Birkenau. And you were placed in a large open room with no roof--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with 50 girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in this point, we were just staying there. And then we, we got out in the morning. They were counting us. Appell was all the time. They were counting us morning, noon, and night. They had nothing there, nothing to wash, nothing to dress. They took everything away from us. Even my shoes that it was so important for me. So all I had is one dress, no underwear. And we got just Holland shoes, you know, this wooden shoes. And once in the day, it came a truck with water. So if you were lucky enough to get a drop of water, you got it. Most of the time, the line was big that we never got there. And once a day they had given us some kind of soup, some kind of water. And that was the whole food that we were getting in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat with?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did we eat with? We had a spoon. The trouble is that by time I went back to my room, I don't-- I didn't always find it. Nobody had names on nothing. So whoever got there first, got it. And who didn't, didn't get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were taking somebody else's. It was like madhouse. And that's-- because when you went to Appell, you couldn't take anything with you. You had to leave. And it happened so fast, that, that you have to run immediately. So that was it. Who were the guards there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all SS mens. There was no other people that I-- mostly was all SS men. They had a blockalteste, but I was not aware of it, who it was or what it was. And that might have been one of us, or I don't know, who took care of the blocks. At that point, in the beginning, everybody was very quiet. We cried more than did anything else. And we were still hoping, and we were looking to see if the other camp, the other blocks has somebody that you know. I really didn't have anybody that I even know. I was totally separated. Because I left Beled. I went to Szombathely, from Szombathely to Sopron. And when I got out from the-- [INAUDIBLE] from there, there was just nobody near me that I even know. So it was a very difficult time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all Hungarians with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all Hungarians. And they were all nearby towns and, and so on. But still, I just couldn't find anybody. I mean, everybody was bunched in. And everybody was drawn here, and you go right, and you go left. And it was just mixed up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been in Birkenau three to four weeks. And then they took us to Allendorf. Allendorf was an munition--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same way. They took in cattle trains. But when we got to Allendorf, we really got a little bit happier. We got a piece of bread, a dark piece of bread for the road. And they managed to give us water.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we got to Allendorf we thought, oh, gosh, who knows what we got? We finally get a bed to sleep on. It wasn't a bed, but it was boards. It was three layers of boards.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a blanket. And we had a straw pillow to, to sleep on. So we thought we were already in seventh heaven. After all, in Birkenau, it was pouring one night. We were all soaked. Every one of us were wet. And we had that much room. One was next to the other. And god forbid to have to go to the bathroom. I mean, we didn't even mind if we had to just go a little bit. But when you have to make a bowel movement, forget it. It was a [INAUDIBLE]. And in Allendorf, we had a washroom where we could go and just wash our hands and faces. We still had only one dress. But every so often, they took that one dress, and they washed it. And they bought us new one. They changed us over. And in the end-- I was very upset, I remember, when they're doing that. Because I had a nice long dress. And it covered at least half of my legs. And the second time when I got a new one, it was a short one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't like that. And somehow, I managed to get a longer one again. And I put my initials on it. I put out a thread from our covers. And I managed to put my initials in there. But I wasn't thinking. I was very stupid. And I put on SS. I put it Sari Stern. And the girls were going to kill me. They said, how could you do something like that? So I said, I, I wasn't thinking. It was my initials. And I wanted to get to the dress back. And they said, that was the wrong thing to do. But it was in already.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and somehow, I never got it back. When it went to the cleaner, it didn't get back to me again. So we got shorter dresses at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else, what else do you remember about life at Allendorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: Allendorf, we got up every morning. We stayed zahlappell, which-- where they were counting us. And we went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they feed you before you went to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had empty coffee. And we had one pieces of bread that we have to have for the whole day. When we came back at night after eight hours work, we would get a plate of soup. And sometime they had a, a piece of salami. But they kept on saying that it's made from horses. And I wouldn't eat it, because it wasn't kosher. So I would change it for a piece of margarine with somebody else. So I had some margarine to put on my bread. And this was the whole food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are many girls who tried to stay kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: We must have had, in, in my bunk, at least 15, 20 of us who really tried to keep everything. We also when we, when we knew it was Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, we started to switch over. Like, we would work one day and one night straight, so we could stay home for the holiday. And the other girls covered for us. We, we had very close relationship to girls who were willing to help us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the girls who were willing to help you out?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ones who didn't care, that figured if their parents died already and this kind of thing didn't meant anything for them. Because they said there was no God. Because God wouldn't let that happen to the good ones. And we weren't the best ones. And we are alive, and they are not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a lot of complaints [INAUDIBLE]. A matter of fact, we had rabbis' daughters there who didn't believe in nothing anymore. What happened to us was either we went one way or we went the other way. Because we didn't know the medium way, you know. It's, it's only because my father was giving us so much lessons towards the end that made me feel like I have to do it. Because I believed that's what they would want me to do. Your father giving you lessons towards the end-- when you were in the last ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the last ghetto he said, a lot of thing going to happen. And he didn't show any good pictures for us what it's going to come for the next few months, he was hoping. But he said, just be careful. And hopefully, we'll get back together where we're supposed to meet. And until then, even if people throw you with stones, you just throw back with bread. You should never do anything that you wouldn't-- that you know that it's not the right thing to do. And we try to-- I try to go very much by the things that he was telling me at that point. And he was really crying. He know that something is going on, that it's going to be not so good. But I didn't know too much about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Allendorf, you worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I worked in a, in a bomb factory. And in the-- towards the end, I was getting hurt over there. So I don't know if you want me to go that far.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, why don't we talk about what you were doing for most of the time in the bomb factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the bomb factory, we had long tables. And on the long tables, girls were standing on one side of the table and on the, on the other side. And we're rolling the bombs into another room, where they were making holes, like that size of hole, to put in the burning stuff, what, what caught, caught onto fire. What did they call that? I can't remember the names anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The material that catches--

speaker: interviewer
text: Catches fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: --fire. Right. So you had to drill that kind of holes in, in the bomb, so they could stuff this in. And many time, I was working in there where they were drilling the holes. But because I didn't want to drink the milk, I used to get yellow right away. They did give you a little bit milk to drink, so the poison in you would go down and clear [INAUDIBLE]. And I hated milk from the home. So I wasn't drinking it. I got yellow most of the time. I'm very light-skinned anyhow. But I used to look yellow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they would throw me out again to the air, not to be there. And a lot of the girls, unfortunately, were working even more dangerous places where they were cooking the poison. And all those girls, I understand, after the war, they had children. And they become not normal, the first one. They had problem, because they took in all this poison. So we really had a hard time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the end, towards the end-- we were there, I think, eight to nine, maybe eight months-- we heard the Americans were flying over. And it used to be an alarm. You know, that they're coming. And we were rolling the bombs. And somehow, my girl who was supposed to hold it cross for me, she ran away because she got scared. We were all running. And the bomb came right on my knee. And I'm still having trouble with it ever since. And then I couldn't walk. It just blew up like that. And they had to take me home in a wagon to-- back to the camp. And I stayed in a room that they called hospital room. But it was only a plain room. There was no doctors there. There was one girl there who was more like a nurse, who knew things, what to put on, and so on. And I was there for a few days. And afterwards, one morning they walked in, and they said, we all have to walk. And you have to just get up. I think we were three or four in the room at that time. Because if you're not going to walk, they're going to kill you right now. They're going to shoot you. Because we just have to leave the place. Because quite often, the airplanes were coming over us. And they were flying very low. You see, where we were working, the bomb factory, I don't know how they even knew that it was the bomb factory. Because it was underground. It was grass growing on the top. We have to go under. But somehow, they find out. And men there started to-- and then in camp, one of the head from the-- it wasn't an SS man. We had Wehrmacht in that camp. He said, you know, you have to walk, no matter how far you could walk. Because I had an order to shoot all of you who cannot walk. I couldn't even stand on my foot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then he came back in a little bit later. And he said, well-- and I didn't understand what he was saying. But in the end what he was saying, I should stay there. Because the other girl knew what he was saying for me not to go out of the room.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, then they came in for me. And they throw me up top on the truck where all the [INAUDIBLE] suitcases were. And one of my friends saw me there. And they said, get up from there. They're going to kill you. They're going to take you to a gas chamber. You must get up. But I couldn't walk. I couldn't move.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they started to walk everybody. And I was on the truck going-- following them. And one night, when it was getting dark already on the same day, the girl stayed in a field where it was only haystacks on the road.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some of them started to hide in different spaces. Or remained because they couldn't walk. So they were on the sidewalk just sitting there waiting for what is going to happen. And then we heard the news that they're going to shoot us tonight. Because this is the end of it. They-- he had an order that-- our head of [INAUDIBLE], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was a Wehrmacht-- that he could shoot everybody. Because he, he doesn't know what to do with us. But he didn't shoot us. He let us just-- five stayed here, and 10 went a little bit further and so on. We all went out on own. And next morning, he came back. And he left a message that he's not going to bother us, and he's not going to shoot us. But he's going to disappear. But he wanted to know that we're not going to bother him, wherever he's going to be, that we're going to just leave him alone. Because he didn't do any harm to us. So that was fine and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody agreed to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was only a few people. We weren't together no more, the 1,000 women. There were all different direction.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still-- how did you get off the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: There, there-- we were slipping in the fields, middle of the night. And that's how everybody disappeared to--

speaker: interviewer
text: So somebody got you off the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody got me off the truck. They're pulling me off the truck. And they made a hole in the haystack. And four girls stayed with me. And they just pushed me in there, because I couldn't walk. And that was that. And then the next morning, somebody came around. And they said that we're not going to do any harm to this SS-- I mean, to this man. Because he was nice to us. And he didn't kill us. He just left us without water, without any food or anything. That if anything comes around, we should sign a paper that we have nothing against him. And I don't remember signing it or not. But in the afternoon, early afternoon, one of the girls looks out. And we heard noises. So the noises that we heard from the haystack were the Americans were marching in. And the first-- one of the girls went out from the haystack to look out. And all she saw was colored people. And she got so scared. She said, this is worse than the Nazis. She doesn't know what they are, but they are dark. They're black. They're like-- I mean, we never saw anything like that, you know. We didn't, we didn't know anything from colored people in Europe. So we got more scared, needless to say. And so we went back again. And we just stayed together. We were shaking with no food, no water, no nothing. This is already the second day. And then all of a sudden, late in the afternoon, we heard some-- a lot of noises. And they were marching. And then another one looked out, because she said there was a different language there. You know, she heard the voices. And then it was the Americans. Well, the first one was Americans, too. But then she realized that there are-- we got American people there. And somehow, one of them saw us. And they must have seen other ones before us that they recognized that we are in trouble and we are the refugees over there. And they started to talk to us. And we-- somehow, we got a Hungarian. He spoke broken Hungarian. But he did speak to us. And he said, we are American. You are liberated. You don't have to worry anymore. We all had the gray dresses. And there-- he must have had experience from before us. And-- so thank God for that. And then he said, stay here. And we're going to come back. Whenever we're going to be stationed someplace around, we're going to come back and look you up. And he came back and started to talk to us. And, and I was telling him the story that I had the addresses from America and from England to look for my family, so I could find my family. So he said well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sari, we had to stop because there was a technical problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about the soldier, the American soldier, who had an, an idea for how you could look for your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was very nice. He tried to explain to us they have a Hungarian paper in America. He's going to write to his parents and puts in all the information and see if we could find the family that way. And that's what he was doing. And he kept on coming back and watch us almost every day, and he--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still in the fields?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We already were placed into-- at that time, we were in the field. But in the evening, they find us an empty hotel. And they put us all in there, whoever was in that particular area. And he kept on coming back to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he came back. And he said, do you know it's Erev Pesach tonight? So we were very excited.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and he went to bring us some food. And we decided that we would eat potatoes and water for the seventh day. Because he couldn't supply us with matzoh or anything like that. And we kept on eating only potatoes, which it turned out to be a very lucky thing in the end. Because all the other girls who got the American food, it was too heavy for their stomachs. And they really got very sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they just fell over, because their stomach wasn't-- Used to?

speaker: interviewee
text: --used to that kind of heavy food. And some of them died. Luckily, I don't remember any of them from our group. But as I was told, people did die, because they were eating fatty food in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go on with what happened while you were in the hotel and afterwards, I'd like to go back a little to your experiences in Allendorf.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who, who where the guards there? And who were the bosses?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the guards were all Wehrmacht, which is not as bad as the SS men. And as long we were doing our work there, they were pretty much left us alone. And basically, the SS men were over them. But we really didn't have too much to do with them. They were on the other side of us in a separate area. They really didn't bother. Every so often, they was-- that somebody-- one of them got sick, an older person. They took her away to someplace. And I don't know if she ever returned. Then it was another girl that I remember who was pregnant. And they took her away to have the baby. And I don't remember her returning at all. And things like that had happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the factory, who were the bosses?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- most of them was Germans, too. And the overseers who looked over us, they were Germans. And they were the ones who were watching us to do the work. But as long we were doing the work, they were not-- we weren't getting beaten. Like in other places, they were hurting us. Only if he didn't want to do anything, then the scream. Screaming went on all the time to make it faster, faster. No matter how fast we were working, it was never fast enough for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any other things you remember about that factory that you want to tell us about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically, just we got up very early in the morning. We went to work. We were freezing all the time. Because wintertime, we have to work. It was-- it's quite a distance from the camp we were walking. And I remember when they were using papers. They were sending us some kind of material to the bomb factory in paper. And we were all grabbing the paper and putting it around our leg. That was keeping us warm, because we had no socks or anything like that. But comparison to Birkenau, that was already a good place. Because every morning we got up. We were able to wash our hands and our faces before we went to work. When we come back, we still could lay on our bed. Nobody was bothering us, so it wasn't-- except that we worked with poisons that the Germans wouldn't work with because-- that's why they put us there. So-- and also, at one point I had trouble with my teeth. So they took me to a, a, a dentist because I was hurting. And they pulled out my three teeth without-- they didn't give me anything. They just pulled it out. And that was the end of that. It wasn't really that probably needed to be pulled out. I maybe needed filling, but there was no such thing. So that was the next best thing. That's, that's about it, what I-- and we were getting the little food in the morning and at night. And that was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you become very close with the other girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yeah. We stick together for a long time. And just the girls from my room, who we stayed together till the very end. But then remember, we get into that hotel. And then one day this American soldier come back and very happy. And that must have been about two weeks later. Because I was able to walk a little bit. And they took me to doctor already.

speaker: interviewer
text: The American soldiers did?

speaker: interviewee
text: American soldier came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: To an American doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: To an-- to a soldier doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I, I think it was a German doctor from the city where we were staying. They were sending us there. And by then, I had a neck like that. My-- all of a sudden, I-- something happened to me. I don't know if I need medication or what, but my neck blew up. And they weren't sure what caused that. But eventually, like in a month or so, it went down. I, I don't know what it was. And at that point, he came back. And he said, I have good news for you. We-- the Americans just liberated Buchenwald. And there was 20,000 men there. So your father must be there. And I was all excited. And I just couldn't wait to get to Buchenwald. And I didn't realize how hard it is to get there. I have no numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I started to walk away from the camp and go towards it the way he told me to go, they arrested me. Because they thought I was running away from something and I'm a German. And it look another Hungarian person somehow to get me out of there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I did get to-- eventually, to Buchenwald when the bodies were still out on the field. And you have to step over them to walk into Buchenwald. Because they were all gunned down on the line. They were still laying out on the fields like that. And I started to look for my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Among the dead bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I couldn't find him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I should stop?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of the tape, yes.
